preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiced palatal approximant /j/ to the voiced alveolar affricate /dz/ is a process of fortition, specifically affrication. This sound change involves an increase in the degree of stricture, where a glide strengthens into a stop-fricative sequence, often accompanied by a shift in the place of articulation from palatal to alveolar.
phoneticEffects: Fortition, Affrication, Place Assimilation (Palatal to Alveolar)

examples.note: The Proto-Indo-European word-initial palatal approximant *i̯- developed into the voiced alveolar affricate /dz/ in many Ancient Greek dialects, represented by the letter ζ (zeta).

sources: Giannakis, G. (Ed.). (2015). Encyclopedia of Ancient Greek Language and Linguistics. Brill.
